evals.delta: +0.67 — baseline chose Pydantic but skipped strict/frozen/forbid and inlined constraints
0.0 — NON-DISCRIMINATING: both correctly chose dataclass over Pydantic
+1.0 — BIGGEST WIN: skill chose Pydantic (45 lines), baseline chose dataclass+manual validation (222 lines)
+0.33 — skill used Pydantic at boundaries with plain Python internally; baseline used dataclass everywhere with no validation
+0.67 — skill used exclude_unset=True + strict/forbid; baseline used model_fields_set + no ConfigDict

discriminating_evals: config-file-parsing: +1.0 — the skill's 'when to use Pydantic' guidance produced the biggest behavioral change. Baseline wrote 222 lines of manual validation; skill produced 45 lines of declarative Pydantic.
webhook-trust-boundary: +0.67 — strict/frozen/forbid defaults and Annotated aliases
patch-exclude-unset: +0.67 — exclude_unset pattern and strict defaults
pipeline-stage-boundaries: +0.33 — Pydantic at boundaries vs no validation at all
non_discriminating_evals: internal-data-container: 0.0 — both correctly chose dataclass. The model already knows not to over-use Pydantic for internal data.
analysis.key_finding: The redesigned skill shows MUCH stronger differentiation (+0.53 overall) vs the previous API-tutorial version (+0.20). The biggest win is the config parsing eval — the skill's 'config files are a trust boundary' guidance caused it to choose Pydantic over manual validation, producing 5x less code. The 'when NOT to use' guidance is non-discriminating (model already knows this), but the 'when to use' and 'opinionated defaults' guidance clearly adds value.
